title: Integer Representation
summary: Unsigned integers, sign-magnitude, binary addition, overflow, ASCII, and Unicode.
content_html:
Integer Representation

A fundamental task for any computer is representing numbers. Since computers only work with bits (0s and 1s), we need conventions for encoding integers in binary form. The choice of representation affects what values are possible, how arithmetic works, and how errors are detected. This lesson covers unsigned integers, early signed representations, binary arithmetic, and character encoding.

Unsigned Integers

Unsigned integers represent only non-negative values. With n bits, you can represent 2n different values, from 0 to 2n−1.

  • 8-bit unsigned: 0 to 255 (28−1)
  • 16-bit unsigned: 0 to 65,535 (216−1)
  • 32-bit unsigned: 0 to 4,294,967,295 (approximately 4.3 billion)
  • 64-bit unsigned: 0 to 18,446,744,073,709,551,615 (approximately 1.8 × 1019)

The binary encoding is straightforward: the bit at position k (counting from 0 at the right) contributes 2k to the value. Unsigned integers are used for memory addresses, array indices, file sizes, network port numbers, and other quantities that are always non-negative.

Sign-Magnitude Representation

To represent negative numbers, the simplest approach is sign-magnitude: reserve the leftmost (most significant) bit as a sign bit (0 = positive, 1 = negative), and use the remaining bits for the magnitude.

Example with 8 bits:

  • +5 = 0000 0101
  • -5 = 1000 0101 (same magnitude, sign bit set to 1)
  • +127 = 0111 1111
  • -127 = 1111 1111

Range for n-bit sign-magnitude: −(2n-1−1) to +(2n-1−1).

Problems with sign-magnitude:

  • Two representations of zero: +0 = 0000 0000 and -0 = 1000 0000. This wastes one code point and complicates equality checks.
  • Complicated arithmetic: To add two sign-magnitude numbers, you must compare signs and either add or subtract magnitudes. Hardware needs extra logic for this.

Because of these drawbacks, sign-magnitude is not used for general integer arithmetic in modern processors (though it is used for the mantissa in floating-point formats).

Binary Addition

Binary addition follows the same rules as decimal addition, but with only two digits:

  • 0 + 0 = 0, carry 0
  • 0 + 1 = 1, carry 0
  • 1 + 0 = 1, carry 0
  • 1 + 1 = 0, carry 1 (sum is 10 in binary)
  • 1 + 1 + 1 = 1, carry 1 (three ones: sum is 11 in binary)

Example: add 45 (0010 1101) and 28 (0001 1100):

  1. Start from the rightmost bit, add column by column
  2. Carry propagates to the left when needed
  3. Result: 73 = 0100 1001

Overflow

Overflow occurs when the true result of an arithmetic operation cannot be represented in the given number of bits. For unsigned n-bit arithmetic, overflow happens when the result exceeds 2n−1. The result wraps around (modulo 2n).

For example, with 8-bit unsigned integers: 200 + 100 = 300, but 300 > 255, so the result stored is 300 − 256 = 44. This is incorrect and is a serious bug if not detected. Hardware typically sets a carry flag when unsigned overflow occurs.

ASCII Character Encoding

ASCII (American Standard Code for Information Interchange) is a 7-bit character encoding that maps 128 symbols to integers 0-127:

  • Digits: ’0’-’9’ = 48-57
  • Uppercase letters: ’A’-’Z’ = 65-90
  • Lowercase letters: ’a’-’z’ = 97-122
  • Space = 32; common punctuation also included
  • Control characters (0-31): non-printable codes like newline (10), tab (9), carriage return (13)

Extended ASCII uses 8 bits, adding characters 128-255 for accented letters and line-drawing characters. Different vendors defined different extended ASCII sets, causing compatibility problems.

Unicode

ASCII only covers English and a few punctuation marks. Unicode is the universal character encoding standard supporting virtually all writing systems in the world, with over 140,000 characters currently defined.

  • Characters are identified by code points (e.g., U+0041 = Latin letter A, U+4E2D = Chinese character for “middle”)
  • UTF-8: Variable-width encoding (1-4 bytes). ASCII characters use 1 byte; other characters use more. Most common encoding on the web.
  • UTF-16: Fixed or variable 2/4 bytes per character. Used internally by Windows and Java.
  • UTF-32: Fixed 4 bytes per character. Simple but memory-intensive.

UTF-8 is backward compatible with ASCII — any valid ASCII file is also valid UTF-8. This property made UTF-8 adoption much easier.

exercises.title: 8-bit Unsigned Range
exercises.prompt: What is the maximum value representable by an 8-bit unsigned integer?

exercises.title: Sign-Magnitude Zero
exercises.prompt: Why is sign-magnitude representation considered problematic?
options: It has two representations of zero
It requires more bits than two's complement
It cannot represent numbers greater than 127
exercises.answer: It has two representations of zero

exercises.title: Binary Addition
exercises.prompt: What is the result of binary addition 1101 + 0011?

exercises.title: ASCII Code
exercises.prompt: What is the ASCII code for the uppercase letter 'A'?
